introduction.p: Green's theorem began with a small rectangle. Opposite sides compared the field in two directions, and the surviving coefficient was Q_x-P_y. After adding many rectangles, every interior edge canceled. What remained was the boundary of the whole region.
That cancellation is the heart of the chapter: \boxed{\int_{\partial R}\omega=\int_R d\omega.} The rest is learning to choose the right 1-form \omega, the right orientation, and the right region.

subsection.title: One theorem, several questions
subsection.p: A boundary curve can answer different questions. The same curve and the same vector field can ask for circulation, flux, or area. Green's theorem helps only after we know which question we are asking.
subsection.md: \begin{array}{c|c|c} \text{Question} \amp \text{Boundary form} \amp \text{Interior form} \\ \hline \text{circulation of } \mathbf F=(M,N) \amp M\,dx+N\,dy \amp (N_x-M_y)\,dx\wedge dy \\[2mm] \text{outward flux of } \mathbf F=(M,N) \amp M\,dy-N\,dx \amp (M_x+N_y)\,dx\wedge dy \\[2mm] \text{area of }R \amp x\,dy \amp dx\wedge dy \\[2mm] \text{area of }R \amp -y\,dx \amp dx\wedge dy \\[2mm] \text{area of }R \amp \dfrac12(-y\,dx+x\,dy) \amp dx\wedge dy \end{array}
subsection.p: The same theorem sits behind every row: \int_{\partial R}\omega=\int_R d\omega. The form \omega decides what the boundary is measuring.

example.title: The same field, two different boundary questions

statement.p: Let R=[0,2]\times[0,1], with positive orientation, and let \mathbf F(x,y)=(M,N)=(xy,\;x^2-y).
The same field and the same rectangle gave two different answers because circulation and flux are different boundary measurements.

subsection.title: A checklist before using Green's theorem
subsection.p: Before applying \int_{\partial R}\omega=\int_R d\omega, pause long enough to check four things.

For a region without holes, positive orientation means counterclockwise. For a region with holes, the outer boundary is counterclockwise and each inner boundary is clockwise. That one rule says the same thing everywhere: \text{as you walk, the region stays on your left.}

subsection.title: Area from a boundary curve
subsection.p: Green's theorem can turn a boundary walk into an area calculation. Since d\left(\frac12(-y\,dx+x\,dy)\right)=dx\wedge dy, a positively oriented simple closed curve C=\partial R satisfies \boxed{\operatorname{Area}(R)=\frac12\oint_C(-y\,dx+x\,dy).}
This formula becomes especially useful when the boundary is known but the region is awkward.

example.title: The shoelace formula from Green's theorem

statement.p: A surveyor records the vertices of a small lot, in counterclockwise order: (0,0),\qquad (4,1),\qquad (5,4),\qquad (2,5),\qquad (-1,3). The boundary is made of straight line segments.

On one segment from (x_i,y_i)\quad\text{to}\quad(x_{i+1},y_{i+1}), write x(t)=x_i+t(x_{i+1}-x_i),\qquad y(t)=y_i+t(y_{i+1}-y_i),\qquad 0\le t\le1. Then dx=(x_{i+1}-x_i)\,dt,\qquad dy=(y_{i+1}-y_i)\,dt. So
\int_{\text{edge}}(-y\,dx+x\,dy) \amp= \int_0^1\Bigl[-(y_i+t\Delta y)\Delta x+(x_i+t\Delta x)\Delta y\Bigr]dt \amp= x_i\Delta y-y_i\Delta x \amp= x_i y_{i+1}-x_{i+1}y_i.
Adding all edges gives \boxed{\operatorname{Area}(R)=\frac12\left|\sum_{i=1}^n(x_i y_{i+1}-x_{i+1}y_i)\right|,} where the last point is followed by the first: (x_{n+1},y_{n+1})=(x_1,y_1). This is the shoelace formula.

For the lot above,
\sum (x_i y_{i+1}-x_{i+1}y_i) \amp= (0\cdot1-4\cdot0) \amp\quad +(4\cdot4-5\cdot1) \amp\quad +(5\cdot5-2\cdot4) \amp\quad +(2\cdot3-(-1)\cdot5) \amp\quad +((-1)\cdot0-0\cdot3) \amp= 0+11+17+11+0 \amp= 39.
Therefore \boxed{\operatorname{Area}(R)=\frac{39}{2}.} A formula often taught as a polygon trick is really Green's theorem in disguise.

subsection.title: A polar area formula from the same boundary form
subsection.p: The same area form also explains the familiar polar area formula.

statement.p:
A drone traces the boundary of a lake whose polar equation is r(\theta)=3+\cos(3\theta),\qquad 0\le \theta\le2\pi. Since r(\theta)\gt 0 for all \theta, the curve does not pass through the origin. The area enclosed is
A \amp= \frac12\int_0^{2\pi}(3+\cos3\theta)^2\,d\theta \amp= \frac12\int_0^{2\pi}\left(9+6\cos3\theta+\cos^2 3\theta\right)d\theta.
Now \int_0^{2\pi}\cos3\theta\,d\theta=0 and \int_0^{2\pi}\cos^2 3\theta\,d\theta=\pi. Therefore
A \amp= \frac12(18\pi+\pi) \amp= \frac{19\pi}{2}.
So \boxed{A=\frac{19\pi}{2}.}

figure.caption: A polar boundary can give area without describing the region by inequalities.

exercises.title: Project: area from a measured boundary curve

introduction.p: A curve can be known from measurements instead of from a clean formula. This project uses Green's theorem to estimate area from boundary data.

Suppose a drone flies once counterclockwise around a pond and records points (x_1,y_1),(x_2,y_2),\ldots,(x_n,y_n) at equally spaced times. Connect consecutive points by straight segments, and connect the last point back to the first.

statement.p: Use \operatorname{Area}=\frac12\left|\sum_{i=1}^n(x_i y_{i+1}-x_{i+1}y_i)\right| to estimate the pond's area.
Explain why the estimate improves as the drone records more points, assuming the boundary is smooth.

Repeat the computation using \operatorname{Area}=\oint_C x\,dy. For a straight segment from (x_i,y_i) to (x_{i+1},y_{i+1}), show that \int_{\text{edge}}x\,dy=\frac{x_i+x_{i+1}}{2}(y_{i+1}-y_i). Then show that the result agrees with the shoelace formula.
Try the method on a boundary given parametrically by x(t)=3\cos t+\cos 2t,\qquad y(t)=2\sin t,\qquad 0\le t\le2\pi. Use \operatorname{Area}=\frac12\int_0^{2\pi}\bigl(x(t)y'(t)-y(t)x'(t)\bigr)\,dt.

conclusion.p: The point is not the formula alone. The point is that area can be recovered from a walk around the edge.

statement.p: Let C be the positively oriented boundary of the rectangle [0,3]\times[0,2]. Compute \oint_C (x^2-y)\,dx+(xy+y^3)\,dy using Green's theorem.
Let C be the positively oriented unit circle. Compute \oint_C (e^x-y)\,dx+(x+\cos y)\,dy.
Let R be the triangle with vertices (0,0),\qquad (3,0),\qquad (0,2). For \mathbf F=(x+y,\;2x-y), compute the outward flux across \partial R.
Let A be the annulus 1\le x^2+y^2\le9. Compute \oint_{\partial A}x\,dy. Remember the orientation of the inner boundary.
Let \omega=(y^2+x)\,dx+(x^2-y)\,dy. Find d\omega. Then compute \int_{\partial R}\omega for R=[0,1]\times[0,2].
Let \alpha=\frac{-y\,dx+x\,dy}{x^2+y^2}. Explain why Green's theorem cannot be applied to \alpha on the unit disk. Then explain why it can be applied on the annulus \frac14\le x^2+y^2\le1.
A closed curve C is oriented clockwise. If \frac12\oint_C(-y\,dx+x\,dy)=-8, what is the area enclosed by C?
Find a 1-form \omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy such that d\omega=5\,dx\wedge dy. Use it to write an area-like boundary formula for 5\operatorname{Area}(R).

subsection.title: What Green's theorem cannot yet do
subsection.p: Green's theorem lives in the plane. Its region R is flat, and its boundary \partial R is a curve. The positive orientation rule is simple: walk so the region stays on your left.

But many boundaries in space are not edges of flat regions. A wire loop may bound a soap film. The film may bend. The same wire may bound many different surfaces. A dome has a top side and a bottom side. A sphere has an outside and an inside.

On a flat region, orientation could be described by left and right. On a surface in space, orientation becomes a choice of normal direction. Once that choice is made, the boundary direction has to match it.

That is the next problem. To carry Green's theorem into space, we need to understand surfaces, their area, their two possible sides, and how a boundary curve knows which side has been chosen. The surprising part is that the same short formula will survive: \int_{\partial(\text{something})}\omega=\int_{\text{something}}d\omega. The something will no longer be only a flat region. It will be a surface.
